We need to remediate the audit rule. The plan: check if augenrules loads rules or auditctl directly. Approaches: check /etc/audit/auditd.conf maybe? By default, auditd uses /etc/audit/rules.d. Let's inspect auditd.conf for 'use_augenrules'. If set to 1, it uses augenrules. So we should edit /etc/audit/rules.d/audit_rules_usergroup_modification_shadow.rules ensure rule present. Also ensure file permissions 640 root. Then run /usr/sbin/augenrules --load to reload. Also maybe reload auditd service via systemctl restart auditd. But better just run /usr/sbin/augenrules --load and maybe systemctl reload auditd.

Alternative simpler: just ensure both types are added, but avoid duplication? But risk of double. We can just add in both. It's okay. But earlier attempt considered duplication unsafe. So plan: 1. Check if file exists: read_file /etc/audit/rules.d/audit_rules_usergroup_modification_shadow.rules. If doesn't exist, create empty file using write_file. 2. Use sed -i to comment/uncomment lines and set rule. But we can't run sed in single command on the file; we need one command: sed -i ... file. We should modify the file accordingly.

Prefer idempotence: If rule exists (uncommented) skip. If commented, uncomment and ensure correct arguments. So sed script: sed -i -e '/^#\?[^#]*\/etc\/shadow/ c\ -w /etc/shadow -p wa -k audit_rules_usergroup_modification' file. That will replace any line containing /etc/shadow. But need ensure only one line. That may replace multiple. But okay.

Then set file permissions root: chmod 640 file. Also ensure owner root: chown root:root file.

Commands: run_cmd 'chmod 640 /etc/audit/rules.d/audit_rules_usergroup_modification_shadow.rules'; run_cmd 'chown root:root /etc/audit/rules.d/audit_rules_usergroup_modification_shadow.rules'; run_cmd '/usr/sbin/augenrules --load'; Or 'systemctl reload auditd'.

Also verify rule active: run_cmd 'auditctl -l | grep audit_rules_usergroup_modification'. But we can't confirm; but can run.

However, we might need to check OS path for augenrules: /usr/sbin/augenrules likely present. Let's plan to run it; if fails, fallback to restart auditd.

Will use sed -i to ensure rule. We'll add to file if not present.

We also need to ensure that file is not duplicated. Use sed to replace any line that matches pattern: '/-w \/etc\/shadow/'. Let's use: sed -i -e '/-w \/etc\/shadow\>/c\ -w /etc/shadow -p wa -k audit_rules_usergroup_modification' file. This will replace entire line.

But "||" counts as chain? Not explicitly forbidden. Only prohibits && or ;. Let's avoid.

Better: separate steps: open file for rewrite with sed to replace lines, then append rule using 'tee -a file << EOF ...' but that requires bash with heredoc, which uses ; as part of command? Not sure. Maybe simpler: use 'cat <<EOF >>file' but that also involves shell features.

But we cannot know failure on shell side. Might still duplicate. So risk duplication.

Alternative: always apply sed replacement: 'sed -i -e "/^#\?[^#]*\/etc\/shadow/c\ -w /etc/shadow -p wa -k audit_rules_usergroup_modification" file'. If line exists, replaced. If not, nothing happens. After that, append rule: 'echo " -w /etc/shadow -p wa -k audit_rules_usergroup_modification" >> file', but may duplicate if rule existed. But do not check. But risk duplication.

We could overwrite file content to ensure only rule present: we can overwrite file with rule: run_cmd 'printf " -w /etc/shadow -p wa -k audit_rules_usergroup_modification\n" > /etc/audit/rules.d/audit_rules_usergroup_modification_shadow.rules', but might remove other rules, not desirable.

Better to not risk duplication, we can remove duplicate lines via sed before appending. Use: sed -i '/-w \/etc\/shadow/d' file; then echo rule >> file. That ensures no duplicates. So plan: run_cmd 'sed -i "/-w \\\/etc\\/shadow/d" /etc/audit/rules.d/audit_rules_usergroup_modification_shadow.rules'; then run_cmd 'echo " -w /etc/shadow -p wa -k audit_rules_usergroup_modification" >> /etc/audit/rules.d/audit_rules_usergroup_modification_shadow.rules'.

We can also use sed to ensure rule exists: sed -i '/-w \/etc\/shadow/!a -w /etc/shadow -p wa -k audit_rules_usergroup_modification' to add if missing. That uses ! pattern. That ensures only one appended. That may be more elegant. So run_cmd 'sed -i "/-w \\/etc\\/shadow/!a -w /etc/shadow -p wa -k audit_rules_usergroup_modification" file'. That will add rule if not present. And if present, might duplicate. But we can delete all duplicate lines first, then add.
